title: The Fifth Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sermon Date: February 6, 2022 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Fifth Sunday after the Epiphany falls within the season of Epiphany (January 6 - Ash Wednesday), a time focused on Christ's manifestation and revelation. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would likely be Isaiah 40:21-31, Psalm 147:1-11, 20c, 1 Corinthians 9:16-23, and Mark 1:29-39, continuing the Epiphany theme of Christ's power and ministry. This Sunday comes two weeks before Ash Wednesday (March 2, 2022), marking the transition toward the penitential season of Lent.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating the post-pandemic reopening of in-person worship with significant variation in diocesan policies regarding masking and vaccination. The Diocese of Colorado had recently clarified its vaccination guidance for clergy and lay leaders, though individual parishes maintained some autonomy in implementation. No major denominational controversies or General Convention actions were active in early February 2022.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced a severe winter weather event in late January 2022 with significant snowfall, and February 6 fell during a period of cold, dry conditions typical of the Front Range winter. Saint John's Cathedral sits in downtown Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood, an area experiencing ongoing discussions about urban density, homelessness, and post-pandemic commercial recovery. Colorado continued tracking COVID-19 cases with the Omicron variant dominant at this time, though hospitalizations were beginning to decline from peak levels.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was in the final weeks before Russia's invasion of Ukraine (February 24, 2022), with intensive diplomatic efforts and media coverage of military buildups dominating news cycles. The Biden administration was facing inflation concerns and midterm election messaging as 2022 approached. Major League Baseball remained in labor dispute negotiations (the MLB lockout would lead to game cancellations), and the Winter Olympics in Beijing were ongoing (February 4-20, 2022), with American athletes competing amid diplomatic tension over China's human rights record.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia maintained troops at the Ukrainian border with Western intelligence agencies warning of imminent invasion plans, though diplomatic channels remained technically open. The Beijing Winter Olympics were in their early days with various nations participating while diplomatic boycotts by the US and others proceeded. COVID-19 continued as a significant global health issue with new variants emerging and varying vaccination rates across countries.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture was experiencing collective uncertainty and wariness following two years of pandemic disruption, with return to normal feeling incomplete and fragmented by regional variations in policy. There was palpable anxiety about the potential Ukraine invasion in mainstream American consciousness, though not yet the crisis that would dominate March onward. The Super Bowl (February 13) was one week away, representing a return to major public gatherings and a moment of national cultural focus. Spiritually, many churches were finding their rhythm again with in-person worship, though attendance had not fully recovered to pre-pandemic levels, and congregations remained marked by unresolved COVID-related tensions and divisions.
